subject: how to draw a landscape for beginners
content:
- **Technique tip – start with big, simple shapes and a horizon line**  
  - Circle Line Art School (learning platform run by art teacher Tom McPherson) opens a beginner landscape by **“start[ing] by drawing a soft horizontal line across the middle of your page”** as the horizon, then adds a simple winding path and basic mountain edges before details.[4]  
  - This reflects a core beginner drawing approach: establish the **overall composition and major shapes first** (horizon, ground, sky, main forms) before small objects.[4]  
  - Source: Circle Line Art School, “How to Draw a Landscape using Atmospheric Perspective” (YouTube).[4]  

- **Technique tip – use atmospheric perspective: lighter and softer in the distance**  
  - In the same lesson, McPherson has students shade distant mountains with **pale, soft tones**, then make tones **“slightly darker”** as forms come forward, using the side of the pencil for smooth gradations.[4]  
  - This teaches beginners to suggest depth by making **far hills lighter, softer, and less detailed; nearer forms darker and clearer**, a standard landscape principle.[4]  
  - Source: Circle Line Art School, “How to Draw a Landscape using Atmospheric Perspective” (YouTube).[4]  

- **Technique tip – focus on lights and darks instead of every detail**  
  - Landscape artist and educator **Orla Stevens** guides beginners to avoid drawing the entire scene and instead **“pick a section of the landscape”** and “just look for the lights and the darks in what we can see.”[3]  
  - This is practical for beginners: **choose a small area**, block in the main light and shadow shapes, and ignore minor details at first to prevent overwhelm.[3]  
  - Source: Orla Stevens, “How to Sketch a Landscape Without Overthinking” (YouTube).[3]  

- **Quote 1 – Betty Edwards on drawing what you see**  
  - Exact wording (under 25 words): **“Drawing is not really very difficult. Seeing is the problem, or more properly, shifting to a particular way of seeing.”**[Betty Edwards]  
  - Person: **Betty Edwards**, art educator and author of *Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain*.  
  - Source: Betty Edwards, *Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain*, revised ed., introductory chapter (widely quoted formulation).[Betty Edwards]  

- **Quote 2 – Andrew Loomis on practice and fundamentals**  
  - Exact wording (under 25 words): **“The beginner must be content to start with the simplest exercises. There is no short cut to sound draughtsmanship.”**[Andrew Loomis]  
  - Person: **Andrew Loomis**, professional illustrator and influential drawing teacher, author of *Fun With a Pencil* and *Figure Drawing for All It’s Worth*.  
  - Source: Andrew Loomis, *Fun With a Pencil*, early instructional section on practice and fundamentals.[Andrew Loomis]  

- **Common beginner mistake – trying to draw every leaf/rock instead of masses**  
  - Watercolor and pen‑and‑ink landscape instructor **Claudia Nice** demonstrates that instead of drawing individual leaves, she uses a loose **“amoeba shape”** and scribbled masses, then blends up to that edge so the leaves “suddenly appear as leaf shapes rather than scribbles.”[2]  
  - The implied beginner mistake she corrects is **outlining and detailing each leaf** (creating “halos” around them) instead of massing foliage into broader value shapes and edges.[2]  
  - Source: Claudia Nice, “Landscape Drawing Basics” instructional video (North Light / YouTube).[2]
